items.statement: Screening for skin cancer has been proven to decrease mortality through prospective randomized studies.
items.explanation: No prospective randomized study has been performed to look for a mortality decrease in skin cancer screening.
items.reference: Prevention and Early Detection of Cancer · CANCER SCREENING (part 6)

items.statement: MCED tests have extensive evidence supporting their use for asymptomatic individuals from outcomes data.
items.explanation: Most evidence for MCED tests comes from people already diagnosed with cancer, not from screening in asymptomatic individuals.
items.reference: Prevention and Early Detection of Cancer · CANCER SCREENING (part 6)

items.subtopic: Prevention and Early Detection of Cancer | EDUCATION AND HEALTHFUL HABITS | SMOKING CESSATION
items.assertion: Counseling adolescents is critical to prevent tobacco use.
items.reason: More than 80% of adult smokers begin smoking before the age of 18 years.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why counseling adolescents is important.
items.reference: Prevention and Early Detection of Cancer · Prevention and Early Detection of Cancer | EDUCATION AND HEALTHFUL HABITS | SMOKING CESSATION

items.subtopic: Prevention and Early Detection of Cancer | EDUCATION AND HEALTHFUL HABITS | SMOKING CESSATION
items.assertion: Electronic cigarettes have been conclusively proven to reduce health risks compared to traditional smoking.
items.reason: The net effects of electronic cigarettes on health are poorly studied.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the passage states that the net effects of electronic cigarettes on health are not well understood.
items.reference: Prevention and Early Detection of Cancer · Prevention and Early Detection of Cancer | EDUCATION AND HEALTHFUL HABITS | SMOKING CESSATION

items.assertion: Dietary fat is conclusively proven to cause cancer.
items.reason: International ecologic studies suggest that diets high in fat are associated with increased risk for certain cancers.
items.explanation: Despite correlations, dietary fat has not been proven to cause cancer.
items.reference: Prevention and Early Detection of Cancer · PHYSICAL ACTIVITY | DIET MODIFICATION

items.assertion: Hormonal therapy with estrogen plus progestin is recommended as a standard preventive agent for colorectal cancer.
items.reason: The Women's Health Initiative showed a 44% lower relative risk of colorectal cancer in postmenopausal women taking these hormones compared to placebo.
items.explanation: Despite the reduced risk, the modest increase in cardiovascular and breast cancer risks associated with hormonal therapy means it is not recommended as a standard preventive agent.
items.reference: Prevention and Early Detection of Cancer · CHEMOPREVENTION OF COLON CANCER

items.subtopic: VACCINES AND CANCER PREVENTION | SURGICAL PREVENTION OF CANCER
items.assertion: Prophylactic bilateral mastectomy significantly reduces breast cancer-related deaths.
items.reason: Postmastectomy breast cancer-related deaths were 81–94% lower in high-risk women compared with sister controls.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Prevention and Early Detection of Cancer · VACCINES AND CANCER PREVENTION | SURGICAL PREVENTION OF CANCER

items.subtopic: VACCINES AND CANCER PREVENTION | SURGICAL PREVENTION OF CANCER
items.assertion: Prophylactic oophorectomy is associated with a decreased risk of cardiovascular disease.
items.reason: An emulated target trial showed an increased risk for cardiovascular disease when done in premenopausal women.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true; oophorectomy is associated with increased cardiovascular risks.
items.reference: Prevention and Early Detection of Cancer · VACCINES AND CANCER PREVENTION | SURGICAL PREVENTION OF CANCER

items.assertion: Overdiagnosis is the detection of pseudo disease.
items.reason: Many slow-growing tumors fulfill histologic criteria for cancer but will never become clinically significant or cause death during a patient's remaining life span.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains overdiagnosis as detecting pseudo disease.
items.reference: Prevention and Early Detection of Cancer · CANCER SCREENING (part 1)

items.assertion: Lead-time bias can reduce cause-specific mortality.
items.reason: Lead-time bias occurs because a test influences the natural history of the disease, prolonging life expectancy.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; lead-time bias does not influence the natural history of the disease or reduce cause-specific mortality.
items.reference: Prevention and Early Detection of Cancer · CANCER SCREENING (part 1)

items.assertion: Screening for specific cancers can lead to overdiagnosis and overtreatment.
items.reason: The diagnosis and treatment of cancers that would never have caused medical problems can lead to unnecessary treatment and patient anxiety.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Prevention and Early Detection of Cancer · CANCER SCREENING (part 2)

items.assertion: The strongest evidence for a screening test comes from studies showing improved survival rates.
items.reason: Studies showing improved survival rates are considered weaker evidence compared to cause-specific mortality reduction.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the strongest evidence comes from cause-specific mortality, not just improved survival rates.
items.reference: Prevention and Early Detection of Cancer · CANCER SCREENING (part 2)

items.assertion: Mammography is less sensitive at detecting breast cancers in women carrying BRCA1 and BRCA2 mutations.
items.reason: Such cancers occur in younger women, where mammography is known to be less sensitive.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why mammography is less sensitive for these women.
items.reference: Prevention and Early Detection of Cancer · CANCER SCREENING (part 3)

items.assertion: Screening more frequently than recommended adds significant benefit to cervical cancer screening.
items.reason: Frequent screening leads to unnecessary procedures and overtreatment of transient lesions.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; frequent screening does not add significant benefit but can lead to harm.
items.reference: Prevention and Early Detection of Cancer · CANCER SCREENING (part 3)

items.assertion: One-time colonoscopy detects more advanced lesions than one-time FOBT with sigmoidoscopy.
items.reason: Colonoscopy is a more invasive procedure compared to FOBT with sigmoidoscopy.
items.explanation: The assertion is true because colonoscopy detects ~25% more advanced lesions, but the reason does not explain why it detects more advanced lesions.
items.reference: Prevention and Early Detection of Cancer · CANCER SCREENING (part 4)

items.assertion: CT colonography has a comparable sensitivity to colonoscopy for detecting polyps ≥6 mm.
items.reason: CT colonography is performed at expert centers, which ensures high-quality imaging.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why CT colonography has comparable sensitivity to colonoscopy.
items.reference: Prevention and Early Detection of Cancer · CANCER SCREENING (part 4)

items.assertion: PSA screening for prostate cancer has been shown to significantly reduce mortality.
items.reason: Randomized trials indicate that the effect of PSA screening on prostate cancer mortality across a population is, at best, small.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because randomized trials show only a small effect on mortality.
items.reference: Prevention and Early Detection of Cancer · CANCER SCREENING (part 5)

items.assertion: Low-dose CT screening for lung cancer reduces the risk of dying from lung cancer.
items.reason: The NLST demonstrated about 3 fewer deaths per 1000 people screened with CT compared to chest x-ray after 12 years.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Prevention and Early Detection of Cancer · CANCER SCREENING (part 5)
